coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the quantum yield and Cu2+ sensing sensitivity of carbon dots based on the nano-space confinement effect of silica matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Yali
Liu, Chunyan
Zheng, Xingwang
coredata.dc:description: Although the analytical applications of the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were widely explored, the development of effective fluorescence signal amplification scheme based on the CDs was challengeable. In this work, we firstly found that, after CDs and polyethyleneimine (PEI) were together doped into silica nanoparticles via a reverse microemulsion route, the CDs inside as-prepared CDs/PEI/silica nanoparticles (CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs) presented the higher fluorescence quantum yield and stronger signal amplification ability than that of free-state CDs. Here, the possible fluorescence enhancing mechanism of CDs inside CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was investigated by UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, IR spectra, transmission electron microscope and zeta potential technique and the possible mechanism was also proposed. In addition, the Cu2+ was selected as the model to investigate the signal amplification feature of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs. Our results showed that, encapsulation of CDs and PEI into silica NPs lead to a fluorescence enhancement of CDs, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs increased from 13.7% to 38.6%. The as-prepared CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs exhibited 100-times sensitivity increasing for sensing Cu2+ compared with free-state CDs sensing scheme reported by the previous literature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silica nanomatrix
Carbon dots
Fluorescence enhancement
Signal amplification
Cu2+ sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of fluorescent PEI-passivated CDs
The interaction of the CDs with PEI under the nano-sol-gel procedure
Fluorescence signal amplification sensing of copper ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21375085 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2016CBZ003 ). We thank Darlene Schmidt for her kind help on our paper writing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the process of the selective interaction of silica nanomatrix towards the different amine groups of PEI.
Illustration for the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of PEI to CDs inside silica matrix.
TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of CDs/SiO2 NPs; TEM images of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs prepared by different PEI concentration: (c) 4 mg mL−1; (d) 20 mg mL−1; (e) 40 mg mL−1; (f) 80 mg mL−1.

Corresponding fluorescence spectra of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs prepared by different PEI concentration (insert plot described the corresponding fluorescence signals of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs prepared by different PEI concentration); PEI concentration was: (a) 0; (c) 4 mg mL−1; (d) 20 mg mL−1; (e) 40 mg mL−1; (f) 80 mg mL−1.

Fluorescence signals change of CDs inside the water droplet of the reverse microemulsion system: (a) in the presence of only 50 μL of free-state PEI without addition of the TEOS; (b) in the presence of both of 50 μL of free-state PEI (40 mg mL−1) and 70 μL of TEOS.

(A) Fluorescence spectra change of CDs with reaction time increasing in the presence of 40 mg mL−1 of additional PEI inside the water droplet of the reverse microemulsion system without addition of the TEOS; (B) Fluorescence spectra change of CDs with reaction time increasing under the nano-sol-gel procedure in the presence of free-state PEI (50 μL, 40 mg mL−1) and 70 μL of TEOS (right).

(A) Absorption spectra of the free-state CDs (black) and CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs (red). (B) Emission spectra of free-state CDs (black) and CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs (red). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra change of CDs/PEI/SiO2 upon addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ (in 20 mM pH 6.0 Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4 (PB) buffer). The concentration of Cu2+ from curve a to curve q was 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.8, 3.8, 5.8, 7.8, 9.8, 30, 50, 100, 300, 500,700 and 900 nM, respectively. Inset: Linear fitting curve of F0/F versus a different concentration of Cu2+ (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.8, 3.8, 5.8, 7.8, 9.8 nM), where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs in the absence and presence of Cu2+.

Stern–Volmer plot of free-state CDs (a) and CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs (b) describing the response of F0/F to the concentration of Cu2+ (in 20 mM pH 6.0 PB buffer). F0 was initial fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence of Cu2+; F represented fluorescence intensities of CDs at a given concentration of Cu2+. The concentration of Cu2+ was 1.8, 3.8, 5.8, 7.8 and 9.8 nM, respectively.

Selectivity of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs for sensing of Cu2+ over other representative metallic ions in pH 4.0 NaAc-HAc solution. (the concentrations of Cu2+ was 1.0 × 10−7 M, other metallic ions was 1.0 × 10−6 M).
QYs of CDs and CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs. ((with quinine sulfate (ΦF = 0.52 in 0.05 M H2SO4) as the standard)).

head.ce:title: Enhancing the quantum yield and Cu2+ sensing sensitivity of carbon dots based on the nano-space confinement effect of silica matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an 710119, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although the analytical applications of the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were widely explored, the development of effective fluorescence signal amplification scheme based on the CDs was challengeable. In this work, we firstly found that, after CDs and polyethyleneimine (PEI) were together doped into silica nanoparticles via a reverse microemulsion route, the CDs inside as-prepared CDs/PEI/silica nanoparticles (CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs) presented the higher fluorescence quantum yield and stronger signal amplification ability than that of free-state CDs. Here, the possible fluorescence enhancing mechanism of CDs inside CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was investigated by UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, IR spectra, transmission electron microscope and zeta potential technique and the possible mechanism was also proposed. In addition, the Cu2+ was selected as the model to investigate the signal amplification feature of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs. Our results showed that, encapsulation of CDs and PEI into silica NPs lead to a fluorescence enhancement of CDs, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs increased from 13.7% to 38.6%. The as-prepared CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs exhibited 100-times sensitivity increasing for sensing Cu2+ compared with free-state CDs sensing scheme reported by the previous literature.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescence signal amplification strategy, as one of the most promising directions to achieve the detection of the ultralow target species, has attracted tremendous attention [1–5]. For producing a novel signal amplification gain in response to ultralow analytes, different strategies such as the design and synthesis of excellent fluorophore molecular probes [6,7], sensing analytes by using conjugated polymers [5,8,9], incorporation of nanomaterials to increase loading of tags [1,10,11], etc. [12] have been reported.

Among these strategies, the silica nanomaterials-based fluorescence signal amplification platform [11,13–18] has been given much attention due to their advantages of easy synthesis and versatility. In this regard, the fluorescent dye-doped silica nanoparticles are of most popular since single silica nanoparticle could dope lots of dye molecules and present the great signal amplification ability [15,18]; however, the leaking effect of dye from silica particles and the limited photo-stability of dye molecules in silica matrix make these strategy challengeable in practical applications. Similarly, another paralleled widely applied fluorescence signal amplification scheme was developed by using dye-organized silica nanoparticles because they could sense the single target by the collective effect of the pre-organized fluorescence dyes [12,14]. However, the complicated chemical modification procedures for grafting dyes onto the surface or inside silica nanoparticles and the limited number of dye molecules for generating collective effect make this scheme challenged for highly sensitivity purpose. Thus, development of a new effective signal amplification scheme using silica nanoparticle is still desirable.

On the other hand, as new fluorescent nanomaterials, the carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention in the past years due to their eminent properties such as chemical inertness, excellent photo-stability, tunable surface functionalities, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [19–21]. Based on these outstanding characteristics, the fluorescent CDs have been widely used in bioimaging [22–24], metal ions detection [25,26], catalysis [27] and photovoltaic devices [28,29] applications. However, up to now, to the best of our knowledge, no effective scheme was reported to explore the fluorescence signal amplification feature of CDs.

Herein, for the first time, we found that: after the PEI and CDs were together doped into silica nanoparticles via a reverse microemulsion route, the densely concentrated amine groups of PEI could greatly increase the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs inside silica nanoparticles based on the nano-space confinement effect of rigid silica matrix and the supramolecular interaction of these amine groups of PEI with carboxyl groups of CDs. More importantly, compared to free-state CDs, based on the space confinement effect of silica matrix, the CDs inside as-prepared CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs presented the excellently fluorescence signal amplification ability to sense copper ions (Cu2+). Our results showed that, compared to free-state CDs, except for about the 2.3 times quantum yield enhancing, the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs presented a 100 times sensitivity increasing for sensing Cu2+. In addition, compared to the dye pre-organized silica NPs signal amplification sensing scheme, the proposed scheme possessed the following advantages: firstly, the procedures for preparation of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was very simple, they could be obtained by a one-pot method through a W/O microemulsion route; secondly, because the good photo-stability and the relatively large size of CDs, the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs presented the excellent photo-stability and avoided the leaking effect of the fluorophores from silica NPs.

Triton X-100 (TX-100), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 99.9%), polyethyleneimine with molecular weight 600 Da (PEI, 99.9%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (United States); cyclohexane, acetone, ethanol and ammonium hydroxide (25–28 wt%) were obtained from Xian Chemical Reagent Factory; citric acid was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China); n-hexanol was purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Factory; a stock solution of Cu(NO3)2 (0.01 M) was prepared, and then various concentrations were obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution; ultrapure water of which obtained from Milli-Q system (Millipore, 18.2 MΩ) was used in all experiments. All other reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.

A multiposition magnetic stirrer (IKA, Germany) was used for preparation of nanoparticles. Nanoparticles were collected by a high-speed centrifuge (5804R, Eppendorf, Germany). A Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI in America) was used to characterize the morphology of nanoparticles. F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to measure fluorescence spectra of nanoparticles. UV–vis absorption spectra were characterized by an UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of fluorescent PEI-passivated CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PEI-passivated CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal pyrolysis method according to the literature [26]. Briefly, 1.0 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of water and then was transferred into a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then, 5 mL of PEI solution (0.1 g mL−1) was added under stirring, the mixture of PEI and citric acid was heated in an oven for 90 min (150 °C). The colorless mixture of PEI and citric acid turned into brown after being heated, suggesting the formation of CDs. Finally, the obtained CDs was purified by dialyzing against ultrapure water through dialysis membranes (Spectrumlabs, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) at 3000 Da cutoff for 48 h.

CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs were synthesized by a one-pot method in room temperature through a reverse microemulsion route [30,31]. Briefly, 60 μL of as-prepared CDs solution and 50 μL of PEI (4–80 mg mL−1) were added to the microemulsion solutions composed of TX-100 (1.77 mL), cyclohexane (7.50 mL), 1-hexanol (1.80 mL) and ultrapure water (300 μL). After the mixture was stirred for 1 h, 70 μL of TEOS and 50 μL of NH4OH (25%−28%) were added. The ultimate mixture was stirred for 24 h to allow CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs formation. Then 6 mL of acetone was added to break the microemulsion and release the nanoparticles. The resultant particles were separated from the reaction mixture by centrifugation for 15 min (8000 rpm). The obtained CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs were washed two times with ethanol and three times with water.

The TEM technique and the fluorescence spectra were used to characterize the synthesized CDs. The HRTEM results (as shown in Fig. 1 ) revealed that the sphere shape CDs were formed with sizes about 5 nm. Then, the fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths were recorded. The results showed that, while the excitation wavelengths were red-shifted from 320 to 400 nm, the emission wavelengths of CDs changed from 464 to 485 nm gradually. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of CDs was at about 360 nm and 475 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior was similar to that of other CDs [32,33] which suggested that the fluorescent CDs were successfully synthesized.

In our initial test, the CDs/SiO2 NPs were obtained by directly doping the synthesized PEI-passivated CDs into the silica nanoparticles via a reverse microemulsion route since the PEI-passivated CDs with positive charges were easily doped into silica nanoparticles. The morphology of CDs/SiO2 NPs was characterized by TEM. TEM images (Fig. 2 a and b) showed that the uniform CDs/SiO2 NPs were indeed formed and presented about 80 nm sizes. Obviously, many CDs were successfully doped into every silica nanoparticle. However, as measuring the fluorescence intensity of CDs/SiO2 NPs, our result showed that, compared with the free-state CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the same amount CDs inside of the CDs/SiO2 NPs was obviously decreased. The decrease of fluorescence intensity of CDs may result from the inner filter effect of densely doped fluorescent CDs themselves in single nanoparticle [25,34].

For overcoming these drawbacks and preparing bright CDs/SiO2 NPs, our scheme was to introduce more free state PEI into the above synthesis system to synthesize the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs, because the free state PEI could decrease the loading fraction of CDs in single CDs/SiO2 nanoparticle based on their competitive interaction with silica matrix and subsequently greatly decrease the fluorescence inner filter effect of densely CDs inside silica NPs.

As shown in Fig. 2(c–f) and Fig. 3 , our results, including fluorescence spectra and TEM images, showed that, while different free-state PEI concentration was used, the morphologies and fluorescence signals of the resultant CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs were obviously changed. The TEM images in Fig. 2(a–f) showed that, as the free-state PEI concentration in the synthesis systems increased, the amount of CDs in every CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs obviously decreased and the sizes of the resultant nanoparticles also decreased. In the high concentration free-state PEI used, the core-shell structural CDs/PEI/SiO2 nanoparticles were formed and their average sizes were about 40 nm. All these TEM results showed that the introduction of the free-state PEI into the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs system could not only decrease the CDs numbers inside the resultant CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs but also change the structures of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs.

The corresponding fluorescence signals (as shown in the insert of Fig. 3) showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs increased obviously as the free-state PEI concentration increased from 4 to 40 mg mL−1; above 40 mg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs decreased significantly. Thus, 40 mg mL−1 of free state PEI was used to synthesize the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs for the further work. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3 showed that, as the increasing of the free-state PEI concentration, the maximum emission wavelengths of these CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs presented the blue-shift feature. While PEI concentration ranged from 0 to 80 mg mL−1, the maximum emission wavelengths of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs were blue-shifted from 470 to 452 nm gradually. These results suggested that the addition of the free-state PEI not only reduced the CDs number in every CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs, but also strongly interacted with CDs. More importantly, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs inside CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was measured. As shown in Table 1 , our results showed that doped CDs with PEI (40 mg mL−1) into silica nanoparticles, the QY of CDs (13.7%) increased significantly. The QY of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was 38.6%, 3-times higher than CDs. In addition, to confirm that the fluorescence enhancement did not result from the decrease of the inner filter effect of fluorescent CDs themselves in single nanoparticle, a NaOH etching treatment for CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was conducted. While CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs suffered from a NaOH etching treatment, the quantum yield of CDs decreased to 13.2% which was approximately equal to that of free-state CDs (Table 1).

All of these above results (either the TEM or the fluorescence spectra) indicated that the incorporation of the free-state PEI inside CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs play an important role in enhancing the fluorescence performances of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction of the CDs with PEI under the nano-sol-gel procedure
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescence enhancement mechanisms of PEI for CDs inside the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs, the following tests were conducted:

The interaction of PEI with silica nanomatrix was firstly investigated by measuring the ζ potential of the PEI/SiO2 NPs at different pH since PEI contains three kinds of amine groups with different pKa values (pKa 4.5 for primary, 6.7 for secondary, 11.6 for tertiary [35,36], respectively). Our results showed that: while the medium’s pH was changed from 12.0 to 8.0, the ζ potential of the PEI/SiO2 NPs was almost unchanged. However, the ζ potential of NPs obviously increased while the medium’s pH was lower than 6.7 (as shown in Fig. S2). These results indicated that the primary and secondary amine groups on PEI inside PEI/SiO2 NPs possessed the great freedom to be protonated but the tertiary amine groups on PEI were masked by the silica nanomatrix. The possible reasons could be explained as Scheme 1 : during the nano sol-gel procedure, initially, the primary particles with SiO− groups were formed in the W/O microemulsion system by hydrolysis of TEOS [37–39]. At this period, the tertiary amines groups on PEI with positive charges were strongly interacted with the SiO− groups of primary particles and were buried inside the particles matrix, but the primary and secondary amine groups were located on the surface of the primary particles and owned the great freedom [36,40]. As the primary particles grew and clustered further to form porous silica nanoparticles (Fig. S3), the tertiary amine groups were effectively masked into silica nanomatrix, but the primary and secondary amine groups on PEI stayed in the gaps between the primary particles and possessed their freedom [41,42] (as shown in Scheme 1). Thus, while the medium’s pH changed close to the pKa of tertiary amine groups, the charge of the PEI/SiO2 NPs did not change with pH decrease, once the pH was lower than the pKa of primary/secondary amine groups, the ζ potential of the PEI/SiO2 NPs changed significantly due to the protonation of these amine groups with high freedom.

Thirdly, the interaction of CDs with PEI inside silica nanomatrix during nano-sol-gel process was studied. 500 μL of mixture in W/O microemulsion was taken out in a certain time for measuring the fluorescence change. As shown in Fig. 4 (curve b), after 60 μL of CDs and 50 μL of PEI (40 mg mL−1) initially mixed with 70 μL of TEOS, the CDs fluorescence intensity of CDs in reverse microemulsion significantly increased with the time increasing from 0.5 h to 6 h. Above 6 h, their fluorescence intensity reached maximum (about 3-fold higher than that of free-stated CDs) and almost unchanged as time increasing from 6 h to 24 h. Of note, our previous results showed that, while the reverse microemulsion route was used to synthesize the silica nanoparticles, the sol-gel reaction of TEOS need to take 6 h to form the initially sphere-shape silica nanoparticles [30]. So the above results indicated that the silica nanomatrix, produced in the nano-sol-gel procedure of TEOS, could assist the fluorescence enhancement of CDs inside silica nanomatrix. These fluorescence enhancement phenomena may be related to the following aspects: due to the great freedom of additional PEI’s chains in water droplet, the interaction of the amine groups of PEI with CDs surface was too weak, so the fluorescence intensities of CDs in water or even in high concentration PEI solution were weak. However, while these CDs and PEI were presented in the silica-based nano-sol-gel system, due to the selective interaction of silica nanomatrix with PEI as well as the rigid structure, some of the primary and secondary amine groups of PEI should be compacted towards the surface of CDs based on the nano-space confinement effect of silica matrix. In this case, these primary and secondary amine groups were close to the surface of the CDs and thus the supramolecular interaction between carboxyl groups of CDs and amine groups was enhanced. Subsequently, the CDs surface was further effectively functionalized by amine groups to lead to a fluorescence enhancement.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5B except for the fluorescence intensity increase, the maximum emission wavelengths of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs in the W/O reverse microemulsion system showed blue-shifts with the TEOS hydrolysis time increasing. The fluorescence enhancement and the maximum emission wavelengths blue-shifts mainly resulted from the space confinement effect of silica matrix to the additional free-stated PEI, which changed the microenvironment of CDs [14].

To further support the supramolecular interaction between CDs and PEI inside CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs, the following test for the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was conducted. While CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs suffered from a NaOH etching treatment, the quantum yield of CDs decreased to 13.2% which was approximately equal to that of free-state CDs (as shown in Table 1). This result suggested that the interaction of CDs with primary and secondary amine groups on PEI was not a covalent bond reaction and the fluorescence enhancement was indeed induced by silica naomatrix. Except for this interaction investigation, the FT-IR, UV–vis and emission spectra of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs were also measured to confirm the supramolecular interaction. As shown in Fig. 6 A, compared with UV–vis absorption spectra of free-stated CDs (black line), the maximum absorption wavelength of CDs inside CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs (red line), showed about 12 nm of blue-shifts. Meanwhile, the emission spectra (Fig. 6B) showed that, compared with 476 nm maximum emission wavelength of free-state CDs, the maximum emission band of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was located at 458 nm. The 18 nm of blue-shifts mainly resulted from the interaction of CDs with PEI in the silica nanomatrix, which changed the microenvironment of CDs inside of silica matrix. These results indicated that the CDs had interacted with the amine groups of PEI.

FT-IR spectra of PEI, CDs and CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs were measured. As shown in Fig. S4, the IR spectra of CDs showed a peak at 1670 cm−1 resulted from the stretching of CO (carbonyl), a broad band at ∼3320 corresponding to the OH stretching vibration of carboxylic acid, confirming that the carboxylic functional groups exists on the surface of CDs. FT-IR spectra of PEI displayed that the NH absorption occurred at around 3304 cm−1 and the CH2 at 2940 and 2820 cm−1. CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs had characteristic absorption bands of both PEI and CDs (NH or OH of 3310 cm−1, CH2 of 2940 and 2820 cm−1 and CO of 1675 cm−1), however, the NH band of PEI and O−H band of CDs shifted and broadened to 3310 cm−1 after encapsulation into silica matrix, suggesting the interaction of carboxyl groups of CDs with amine groups of PEI. In addition, a strong peak at 1070 cm−1 was assigned to the Si–O–Si asymmetric stretch of silica matrix. Since no new characteristic bands appeared in the IR spectra of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs, it suggests that the interaction of CDs with amine groups of PEI was not a covalent bond reaction.
Based on the all of results mentioned above, the fluorescence enhancement of CDs inside CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs could be described by the following Scheme 2 :

Initially, before starting the nano-sol-gel procedure, most of the amine groups of free-state PEI presented the great freedom; they could not closely reach the CDs surface and effectively functionalize CDs; under the nano-sol-gel procedure, silanol polymerized and formed the silica nanomatrix. In this period, the primary and secondary amine groups of PEI were exposed to the outside of the silica matrix and the tertiary amine groups were masked inside the silica matrix. Due to the space confinement effect as well as the rigid structure of silica nanomatrix, many primary and secondary amine groups were compacted to the surface of the CDs, which could induce the closely supramolecular interaction between primary and secondary amine groups and carboxyl groups of CDs and form an effectively amine groups functionalized surface of CDs. Thus, the silica matrix effect assisted the PEI to enhance the fluorescence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence signal amplification sensing of copper ions
ce:section.ce:para: Since the supramolecular interaction of PEI with carboxyl groups of CDs lead to a fluorescence enhancement of CDs, we supposed that, if the analyte could destroy this supramolecular interaction, a “turn-off” fluorescence sensing scheme could be established. Here, Copper ions were chosen as a model to demonstrate our CDs-based signal amplification scheme because single copper ion could bind with multi-amine groups of which at the surface of CDs inside of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs, which may lead to the excellent signal amplification performances. Furthermore, as one of the indispensable micronutrients to human body, copper plays an important role in human health. Copper ions in abnormal levels can lead to some physiologically disorders. A lack of copper can lead to anemia or pancytopenia, while high concentrations are poisonous and can cause Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, Prion, Menkes, and Wilson’s disease [43,44].

Our results (Fig. 7 ) showed that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs decreased gradually with Cu2+ concentration increasing in the range of 0.1–900 nM. The Stern–Volmer quenching curve in the inset of Fig. 7 showed that as the Cu2+ concentration changed from 0.1 to 9.8 nM, the values of F0/F showed good linear correlation with Cu2+ concentration. The related regression equation was Y = 0.07C + 1.04 (R = 0.99). The Stern−Volmer constant (KSV) was determined to be 7.0 × 107 L mol−1.

In addition, compared to free CDs, the CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs had the higher sensing sensitivity than that of free-state CDs. Fig. 8 showed that, the KSV for Cu2+ quenching the fluorescence of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was 7.0 × 107 L mol−1, about 2.3 times higher than that of free-stated CDs (3.0 × 107 L mol−1). The detection limit of Cu2+ (at a S/N of 3) was calculated to be 0.07 nM, about 100 times lower than the that of free-stated CDs sensing Cu2+ reported by a previous literature [26]. The relatively larger amount of amine groups, which densely concentrated on the surface of CDs, resulted in the fluorescence signal amplification effect of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs. A cooper ion complex with multi-amine groups of PEI could strongly destroy the supramolecular interaction between CDs and amine groups, and could effectively lead to the inner filter effect of complex of Cu2+ with amine groups, thus, the fluorescence of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs was strongly and effectively quenched by Cu2+.

The selectivity of this proposed CDs/PEI/SiO2 sensing system for was studied. The change in fluorescence signals in the presence of other metal ions, including K+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Zn2+ and Ag+ was measured. As shown in Fig. 9 , only Hg2+ has a slight influence on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs in pH 4.0 solution. Ag+, which has similar reactivity to Cu2+ in the complexation with amines, did not influence Cu2+ detection. This may attribute to the fact that the complextion of Ag+ with amines did not form complexes of which have absorption in the emission wavelength range of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs fluorescence spectra [26]. Subsequently, thus, the inner filter effect of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs with the formed silver amine complexes did not occur. As for Ni2+, according to a literature [26], in pH higher than 5.0 solution, Ni2+ may interfere seriously with the detection of Cu2+. However, herein, at pH 4.0 solution, due to the fact that the lower pH may prevent Ni2+ from combining with amines, it has no interference in Cu2+ detection. With the addition of 10 mM NaCl in pH 4.0 HAc-NaAc solution, Hg2+ has no influence on fluorescence of CDs/PEI/SiO2 NPs (As shown in Fig. S5). This might result from the fact that high concentration Cl− formed a complex with Hg2+, which subsequently decreased the concentration of Hg2+ [18].

In summary, in this work, the selective interaction of silica nanomatrix towards the different amine groups on PEI and the close interaction of amine groups of PEI with CDs inside silica nanomatrix were firstly observed and further successfully used to enhance the CDs fluorescence quantum yield. The CDs and PEI doped silica nanoparticles were applied for signal amplification sensing of Cu2+. Although the PEI polymer was selected as the model to investigate this new function of silica nanomatrix, we believe that this new finding may be expanded to other aspects of silica NPs to design the new sensing scheme or chemical functions of silica-based nanomaterials by using other amine groups reagents.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21375085) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2016CBZ003). We thank Darlene Schmidt for her kind help on our paper writing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proximity-dependent protein detection based on enzyme-assisted fluorescence signal amplification
Biosens. Bioelectron.
Multiplexed fluorescence detection of microRNAs based on novel distinguishable quantum dot signal probes by cycle amplification strategy
Sens. Actuators B Chem.
Highly selective and sensitive detection of adenosine utilizing signal amplification based on silver ions-assisted cation exchange reaction with CdTe quantum dots
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yali Qiao is currently studying for her Ph.D. degree under supervision of professor Zheng in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University (PR China). Her research interests focus on the development of optical biosensors and synthesis of silica-based nanomaterials.
Chunyan Liu has completed her bachelor degree in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University (PR China).

Xingwang Zheng is working as a professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University (PR China). His main research fields include: silica-based nanomaterials, optical nanosensors and electrochemistry.
